Hands-on Exercise 21: Choropleth Mapping with R

  • Show All Code
  • Hide All Code
  • View Source
Author

Bao Sihan

1 21.1 Overview

Choropleth mapping is used to visualise areal differences by shading polygons with attribute values. In this exercise, I follow the lecturer’s workflow to build choropleth maps for Singapore planning subzones. Compared with a raw default map, the later sections show how data classification, colour scheme and layout settings can improve readability.

2 21.2 Getting Started

Code 
pacman::p_load(sf, tmap, tidyverse, readr)

3 21.3 Importing Data into R

3.1 21.3.1 Import geospatial data

Code 
mpsz <- st_read("../data/MP14_SUBZONE_WEB_PL.shp")
Reading layer `MP14_SUBZONE_WEB_PL' from data source 
  `C:\SHKnighter\ISSS608-VAA\Hands-on_Ex\data\MP14_SUBZONE_WEB_PL.shp' 
  using driver `ESRI Shapefile'
Simple feature collection with 323 features and 15 fields
Geometry type: MULTIPOLYGON
Dimension:     XY
Bounding box:  xmin: 2667.538 ymin: 15748.72 xmax: 56396.44 ymax: 50256.33
Projected CRS: SVY21

3.2 21.3.2 Import attribute data

Code 
popdata <- read_csv("../data/respopagesextod2011to2020.csv", show_col_types = FALSE)
Code 
knitr::kable(head(popdata, 6))
PA SZ AG Sex TOD Pop Time
Ang Mo Kio Ang Mo Kio Town Centre 0_to_4 Males HDB 1- and 2-Room Flats 0 2011
Ang Mo Kio Ang Mo Kio Town Centre 0_to_4 Males HDB 3-Room Flats 10 2011
Ang Mo Kio Ang Mo Kio Town Centre 0_to_4 Males HDB 4-Room Flats 30 2011
Ang Mo Kio Ang Mo Kio Town Centre 0_to_4 Males HDB 5-Room and Executive Flats 50 2011
Ang Mo Kio Ang Mo Kio Town Centre 0_to_4 Males HUDC Flats (excluding those privatised) 0 2011
Ang Mo Kio Ang Mo Kio Town Centre 0_to_4 Males Landed Properties 0 2011

3.3 21.3.3 Data preparation

To match the lecturer’s example, I prepare the year 2020 table and derive the following variables:

  • YOUNG: age group 0 to 24
  • ECONOMY ACTIVE: age group 25 to 64
  • AGED: age group 65 and above
  • TOTAL: total population
  • DEPENDENCY: (YOUNG + AGED) / ECONOMY ACTIVE
Code 
popdata2020 <- popdata %>%
  filter(Time == 2020) %>%
  mutate(
    age_start = readr::parse_number(AG),
    age_group = case_when(
      age_start < 25 ~ "YOUNG",
      age_start < 65 ~ "ECONOMY ACTIVE",
      TRUE ~ "AGED"
    )
  ) %>%
  group_by(PA, SZ, age_group) %>%
  summarise(POP = sum(Pop), .groups = "drop") %>%
  tidyr::complete(
    PA,
    SZ,
    age_group = c("YOUNG", "ECONOMY ACTIVE", "AGED"),
    fill = list(POP = 0)
  ) %>%
  pivot_wider(names_from = age_group, values_from = POP) %>%
  mutate(
    TOTAL = YOUNG + `ECONOMY ACTIVE` + AGED,
    DEPENDENCY = (YOUNG + AGED) / `ECONOMY ACTIVE`
  ) %>%
  select(PA, SZ, YOUNG, `ECONOMY ACTIVE`, AGED, TOTAL, DEPENDENCY)
Code 
knitr::kable(head(popdata2020, 6))
PA SZ YOUNG ECONOMY ACTIVE AGED TOTAL DEPENDENCY
Ang Mo Kio Admiralty 0 0 0 0 NaN
Ang Mo Kio Airport Road 0 0 0 0 NaN
Ang Mo Kio Alexandra Hill 0 0 0 0 NaN
Ang Mo Kio Alexandra North 0 0 0 0 NaN
Ang Mo Kio Aljunied 0 0 0 0 NaN
Ang Mo Kio Anak Bukit 0 0 0 0 NaN

3.4 21.3.4 Joining attribute data and geospatial data

To make the join work correctly, I standardise the case of the planning area and subzone names first.

Code 
popdata2020 <- popdata2020 %>%
  mutate(
    PA = toupper(PA),
    SZ = toupper(SZ)
  ) %>%
  filter(`ECONOMY ACTIVE` > 0)

mpsz_pop2020 <- left_join(mpsz, popdata2020, by = c("SUBZONE_N" = "SZ"))

4 21.4 Choropleth Mapping using tmap

4.1 21.4.1 Quick choropleth map using qtm()

Code 
tmap_mode("plot")
qtm(mpsz_pop2020, fill = "DEPENDENCY")

Observation: This first map is quick to produce, but the styling is still very basic. The legend and border treatment are good enough for exploration, but not yet presentation quality.

4.2 21.4.2 A cleaner choropleth map using map elements

  • Plot
  • Code
Code 
tm_shape(mpsz_pop2020) +
  tm_polygons(
    "DEPENDENCY",
    fill.scale = tm_scale_intervals(
      style = "quantile",
      n = 5,
      values = "brewer.blues"
    ),
    fill.legend = tm_legend(title = "Dependency ratio")
  ) +
  tm_title("Distribution of Dependency Ratio by planning subzone") +
  tm_layout(frame = TRUE) +
  tm_borders(alpha = 0.5) +
  tm_compass(type = "8star", size = 2) +
  tm_grid(alpha = 0.2) +
  tm_credits(
    "Source: Planning sub-zone boundary from Urban Redevelopment Authority (URA)\nPopulation data from Department of Statistics DOS",
    position = c("left", "bottom")
  )
Code 
tm_shape(mpsz_pop2020) +
  tm_polygons(
    "DEPENDENCY",
    fill.scale = tm_scale_intervals(
      style = "quantile",
      n = 5,
      values = "brewer.blues"
    ),
    fill.legend = tm_legend(title = "Dependency ratio")
  ) +
  tm_title("Distribution of Dependency Ratio by planning subzone") +
  tm_layout(frame = TRUE) +
  tm_borders(alpha = 0.5) +
  tm_compass(type = "8star", size = 2) +
  tm_grid(alpha = 0.2) +
  tm_credits(
    "Source: Planning sub-zone boundary from Urban Redevelopment Authority (URA)\nPopulation data from Department of Statistics DOS",
    position = c("left", "bottom")
  )

Insight: This version looks much closer to a cartographic map. The title, legend, compass and credits make the output easier to interpret and more suitable for reporting.

4.3 21.4.3 Drawing a base map

Code 
tm_shape(mpsz_pop2020) +
  tm_polygons()

4.4 21.4.4 Drawing choropleth map by tm_polygons()

Code 
tm_shape(mpsz_pop2020) +
  tm_polygons("DEPENDENCY")

4.5 21.4.5 Separating fill and border

Code 
tm_shape(mpsz_pop2020) +
  tm_fill("DEPENDENCY") +
  tm_borders(lwd = 0.1, alpha = 0.5)

Observation: When borders are added separately, the subzone boundaries become much clearer, especially in the dense central region.

4.6 21.4.6 Data classification methods of tmap

4.6.1 21.4.6.1 Jenks classification

Code 
tm_shape(mpsz_pop2020) +
  tm_polygons(
    "DEPENDENCY",
    fill.scale = tm_scale_intervals(style = "jenks", n = 5)
  ) +
  tm_borders(fill_alpha = 0.5)

4.6.2 21.4.6.2 Equal classification

Code 
tm_shape(mpsz_pop2020) +
  tm_polygons(
    "DEPENDENCY",
    fill.scale = tm_scale_intervals(style = "equal", n = 5)
  ) +
  tm_borders(fill_alpha = 0.5)

4.6.3 21.4.6.3 Quantile classification

Code 
tm_shape(mpsz_pop2020) +
  tm_polygons(
    "DEPENDENCY",
    fill.scale = tm_scale_intervals(style = "quantile", n = 5)
  ) +
  tm_borders(fill_alpha = 0.5)

Student note: The quantile version spreads areas more evenly across classes, so visually it is easier to compare the subzones. In contrast, equal intervals tend to leave many observations in similar light shades when the distribution is uneven.

4.6.4 21.4.6.4 Fixed breaks

It is a good habit to check summary statistics before deciding custom breaks.

Code 
summary(mpsz_pop2020$DEPENDENCY)
   Min. 1st Qu.  Median    Mean 3rd Qu.    Max.    NA's 
 0.0000  0.6519  0.7025  0.7742  0.7645 19.0000      92
Code 
tm_shape(mpsz_pop2020) +
  tm_polygons(
    "DEPENDENCY",
    breaks = c(0, 0.60, 0.70, 0.80, 0.90, 1.00)
  ) +
  tm_borders(fill_alpha = 0.5)

Observation: With fixed breaks, the legend becomes more policy-friendly because the cut points are chosen deliberately rather than fully determined by the software.

4.7 21.4.7 Colour scheme

4.7.1 21.4.7.1 Green palette

Code 
tm_shape(mpsz_pop2020) +
  tm_polygons(
    "DEPENDENCY",
    fill.scale = tm_scale_intervals(
      style = "quantile",
      n = 5,
      values = "brewer.greens"
    )
  ) +
  tm_borders(fill_alpha = 0.5)

4.7.2 21.4.7.2 Reversed green palette

Code 
tm_shape(mpsz_pop2020) +
  tm_polygons(
    "DEPENDENCY",
    fill.scale = tm_scale_intervals(
      style = "quantile",
      n = 5,
      values = "-brewer.greens"
    )
  ) +
  tm_borders(fill_alpha = 0.5)

Student note: I personally prefer the blue palette here because it looks slightly cleaner. But the reversed palette is useful when I want darker colours to emphasise lower values instead of higher values.

4.8 21.4.8 Map layouts

4.8.1 21.4.8.1 Map legend and title

Code 
tm_shape(mpsz_pop2020) +
  tm_polygons(
    "DEPENDENCY",
    fill.scale = tm_scale_intervals(
      style = "jenks",
      n = 5,
      values = "brewer.greens"
    ),
    fill.legend = tm_legend(title = "Dependency ratio")
  ) +
  tm_borders(fill_alpha = 0.5) +
  tm_title("Distribution of Dependency Ratio by planning subzone\n(Jenks classification)")

4.8.2 21.4.8.2 Map style

Code 
tm_shape(mpsz_pop2020) +
  tm_fill(
    "DEPENDENCY",
    style = "quantile",
    palette = "Greens"
  ) +
  tm_borders(alpha = 0.5) +
  tm_style("classic")

4.8.3 21.4.8.3 Cartographic furniture

Code 
tm_shape(mpsz_pop2020) +
  tm_polygons(
    "DEPENDENCY",
    fill.scale = tm_scale_intervals(
      style = "quantile",
      n = 5,
      values = "brewer.blues"
    ),
    fill.legend = tm_legend(title = "Dependency ratio")
  ) +
  tm_title("Distribution of Dependency Ratio by planning subzone") +
  tm_layout(frame = TRUE) +
  tm_borders(alpha = 0.5) +
  tm_compass(type = "8star", size = 2) +
  tm_grid(alpha = 0.2) +
  tm_credits(
    "Source: Planning Sub-zone boundary from Urban Redevelopment Authority (URA)\nPopulation data from Department of Statistics DOS",
    position = c("left", "bottom")
  )

4.9 21.4.9 Drawing small multiple choropleth maps

4.9.1 21.4.9.1 Multiple values in one aesthetic argument

Code 
tm_shape(mpsz_pop2020) +
  tm_fill(c("YOUNG", "AGED"),
          style = "equal",
          palette = "Blues") +
  tm_layout(legend.position = c("right", "bottom")) +
  tm_borders(alpha = 0.5) +
  tm_style("white")

4.9.2 21.4.9.2 Multiple small maps using tm_polygons()

Code 
tm_shape(mpsz_pop2020) +
  tm_polygons(
    c("DEPENDENCY", "AGED"),
    style = c("equal", "quantile"),
    palette = list("Blues", "Greens")
  ) +
  tm_layout(legend.position = c("right", "bottom"))

4.9.3 21.4.9.3 Group-by variable using tm_facets()

Code 
tm_shape(mpsz_pop2020) +
  tm_fill(
    "DEPENDENCY",
    style = "quantile",
    palette = "Blues",
    midpoint = NA
  ) +
  tm_facets(by = "REGION_N", free.coords = TRUE) +
  tm_layout(
    legend.show = FALSE,
    title.position = c("center", "center"),
    title.size = 1.0
  ) +
  tm_borders(alpha = 0.5)

Final reflection: Across all versions, the same data can give different impressions depending on classification and layout. This exercise reminded me that choropleth mapping is not just about plotting values. It is also about choosing a design that supports a fair visual comparison.

Source Code
---
title: "Hands-on Exercise 21: Choropleth Mapping with R"
author: "Bao Sihan"
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 4
    number-sections: true
    code-fold: true
    code-tools: true
execute:
  echo: true
  warning: false
  message: false
---

## 21.1 Overview

Choropleth mapping is used to visualise areal differences by shading polygons with attribute values. In this exercise, I follow the lecturer's workflow to build choropleth maps for Singapore planning subzones. Compared with a raw default map, the later sections show how data classification, colour scheme and layout settings can improve readability.

## 21.2 Getting Started

```{r}
pacman::p_load(sf, tmap, tidyverse, readr)
```

## 21.3 Importing Data into R

### 21.3.1 Import geospatial data

```{r}
mpsz <- st_read("../data/MP14_SUBZONE_WEB_PL.shp")
```

### 21.3.2 Import attribute data

```{r}
popdata <- read_csv("../data/respopagesextod2011to2020.csv", show_col_types = FALSE)
```

```{r}
knitr::kable(head(popdata, 6))
```

### 21.3.3 Data preparation

To match the lecturer's example, I prepare the year 2020 table and derive the following variables:

-   `YOUNG`: age group 0 to 24
-   `ECONOMY ACTIVE`: age group 25 to 64
-   `AGED`: age group 65 and above
-   `TOTAL`: total population
-   `DEPENDENCY`: `(YOUNG + AGED) / ECONOMY ACTIVE`

```{r}
popdata2020 <- popdata %>%
  filter(Time == 2020) %>%
  mutate(
    age_start = readr::parse_number(AG),
    age_group = case_when(
      age_start < 25 ~ "YOUNG",
      age_start < 65 ~ "ECONOMY ACTIVE",
      TRUE ~ "AGED"
    )
  ) %>%
  group_by(PA, SZ, age_group) %>%
  summarise(POP = sum(Pop), .groups = "drop") %>%
  tidyr::complete(
    PA,
    SZ,
    age_group = c("YOUNG", "ECONOMY ACTIVE", "AGED"),
    fill = list(POP = 0)
  ) %>%
  pivot_wider(names_from = age_group, values_from = POP) %>%
  mutate(
    TOTAL = YOUNG + `ECONOMY ACTIVE` + AGED,
    DEPENDENCY = (YOUNG + AGED) / `ECONOMY ACTIVE`
  ) %>%
  select(PA, SZ, YOUNG, `ECONOMY ACTIVE`, AGED, TOTAL, DEPENDENCY)
```

```{r}
knitr::kable(head(popdata2020, 6))
```

### 21.3.4 Joining attribute data and geospatial data

To make the join work correctly, I standardise the case of the planning area and subzone names first.

```{r}
popdata2020 <- popdata2020 %>%
  mutate(
    PA = toupper(PA),
    SZ = toupper(SZ)
  ) %>%
  filter(`ECONOMY ACTIVE` > 0)

mpsz_pop2020 <- left_join(mpsz, popdata2020, by = c("SUBZONE_N" = "SZ"))
```

## 21.4 Choropleth Mapping using `tmap`

### 21.4.1 Quick choropleth map using `qtm()`

```{r}
tmap_mode("plot")
qtm(mpsz_pop2020, fill = "DEPENDENCY")
```

**Observation:** This first map is quick to produce, but the styling is still very basic. The legend and border treatment are good enough for exploration, but not yet presentation quality.

### 21.4.2 A cleaner choropleth map using map elements

::: panel-tabset
## Plot

```{r}
tm_shape(mpsz_pop2020) +
  tm_polygons(
    "DEPENDENCY",
    fill.scale = tm_scale_intervals(
      style = "quantile",
      n = 5,
      values = "brewer.blues"
    ),
    fill.legend = tm_legend(title = "Dependency ratio")
  ) +
  tm_title("Distribution of Dependency Ratio by planning subzone") +
  tm_layout(frame = TRUE) +
  tm_borders(alpha = 0.5) +
  tm_compass(type = "8star", size = 2) +
  tm_grid(alpha = 0.2) +
  tm_credits(
    "Source: Planning sub-zone boundary from Urban Redevelopment Authority (URA)\nPopulation data from Department of Statistics DOS",
    position = c("left", "bottom")
  )
```

## Code

```{r}
tm_shape(mpsz_pop2020) +
  tm_polygons(
    "DEPENDENCY",
    fill.scale = tm_scale_intervals(
      style = "quantile",
      n = 5,
      values = "brewer.blues"
    ),
    fill.legend = tm_legend(title = "Dependency ratio")
  ) +
  tm_title("Distribution of Dependency Ratio by planning subzone") +
  tm_layout(frame = TRUE) +
  tm_borders(alpha = 0.5) +
  tm_compass(type = "8star", size = 2) +
  tm_grid(alpha = 0.2) +
  tm_credits(
    "Source: Planning sub-zone boundary from Urban Redevelopment Authority (URA)\nPopulation data from Department of Statistics DOS",
    position = c("left", "bottom")
  )
```
:::

**Insight:** This version looks much closer to a cartographic map. The title, legend, compass and credits make the output easier to interpret and more suitable for reporting.

### 21.4.3 Drawing a base map

```{r}
tm_shape(mpsz_pop2020) +
  tm_polygons()
```

### 21.4.4 Drawing choropleth map by `tm_polygons()`

```{r}
tm_shape(mpsz_pop2020) +
  tm_polygons("DEPENDENCY")
```

### 21.4.5 Separating fill and border

```{r}
tm_shape(mpsz_pop2020) +
  tm_fill("DEPENDENCY") +
  tm_borders(lwd = 0.1, alpha = 0.5)
```

**Observation:** When borders are added separately, the subzone boundaries become much clearer, especially in the dense central region.

### 21.4.6 Data classification methods of `tmap`

#### 21.4.6.1 Jenks classification

```{r}
tm_shape(mpsz_pop2020) +
  tm_polygons(
    "DEPENDENCY",
    fill.scale = tm_scale_intervals(style = "jenks", n = 5)
  ) +
  tm_borders(fill_alpha = 0.5)
```

#### 21.4.6.2 Equal classification

```{r}
tm_shape(mpsz_pop2020) +
  tm_polygons(
    "DEPENDENCY",
    fill.scale = tm_scale_intervals(style = "equal", n = 5)
  ) +
  tm_borders(fill_alpha = 0.5)
```

#### 21.4.6.3 Quantile classification

```{r}
tm_shape(mpsz_pop2020) +
  tm_polygons(
    "DEPENDENCY",
    fill.scale = tm_scale_intervals(style = "quantile", n = 5)
  ) +
  tm_borders(fill_alpha = 0.5)
```

**Student note:** The quantile version spreads areas more evenly across classes, so visually it is easier to compare the subzones. In contrast, equal intervals tend to leave many observations in similar light shades when the distribution is uneven.

#### 21.4.6.4 Fixed breaks

It is a good habit to check summary statistics before deciding custom breaks.

```{r}
summary(mpsz_pop2020$DEPENDENCY)
```

```{r}
tm_shape(mpsz_pop2020) +
  tm_polygons(
    "DEPENDENCY",
    breaks = c(0, 0.60, 0.70, 0.80, 0.90, 1.00)
  ) +
  tm_borders(fill_alpha = 0.5)
```

**Observation:** With fixed breaks, the legend becomes more policy-friendly because the cut points are chosen deliberately rather than fully determined by the software.

### 21.4.7 Colour scheme

#### 21.4.7.1 Green palette

```{r}
tm_shape(mpsz_pop2020) +
  tm_polygons(
    "DEPENDENCY",
    fill.scale = tm_scale_intervals(
      style = "quantile",
      n = 5,
      values = "brewer.greens"
    )
  ) +
  tm_borders(fill_alpha = 0.5)
```

#### 21.4.7.2 Reversed green palette

```{r}
tm_shape(mpsz_pop2020) +
  tm_polygons(
    "DEPENDENCY",
    fill.scale = tm_scale_intervals(
      style = "quantile",
      n = 5,
      values = "-brewer.greens"
    )
  ) +
  tm_borders(fill_alpha = 0.5)
```

**Student note:** I personally prefer the blue palette here because it looks slightly cleaner. But the reversed palette is useful when I want darker colours to emphasise lower values instead of higher values.

### 21.4.8 Map layouts

#### 21.4.8.1 Map legend and title

```{r}
tm_shape(mpsz_pop2020) +
  tm_polygons(
    "DEPENDENCY",
    fill.scale = tm_scale_intervals(
      style = "jenks",
      n = 5,
      values = "brewer.greens"
    ),
    fill.legend = tm_legend(title = "Dependency ratio")
  ) +
  tm_borders(fill_alpha = 0.5) +
  tm_title("Distribution of Dependency Ratio by planning subzone\n(Jenks classification)")
```

#### 21.4.8.2 Map style

```{r}
tm_shape(mpsz_pop2020) +
  tm_fill(
    "DEPENDENCY",
    style = "quantile",
    palette = "Greens"
  ) +
  tm_borders(alpha = 0.5) +
  tm_style("classic")
```

#### 21.4.8.3 Cartographic furniture

```{r}
tm_shape(mpsz_pop2020) +
  tm_polygons(
    "DEPENDENCY",
    fill.scale = tm_scale_intervals(
      style = "quantile",
      n = 5,
      values = "brewer.blues"
    ),
    fill.legend = tm_legend(title = "Dependency ratio")
  ) +
  tm_title("Distribution of Dependency Ratio by planning subzone") +
  tm_layout(frame = TRUE) +
  tm_borders(alpha = 0.5) +
  tm_compass(type = "8star", size = 2) +
  tm_grid(alpha = 0.2) +
  tm_credits(
    "Source: Planning Sub-zone boundary from Urban Redevelopment Authority (URA)\nPopulation data from Department of Statistics DOS",
    position = c("left", "bottom")
  )
```

### 21.4.9 Drawing small multiple choropleth maps

#### 21.4.9.1 Multiple values in one aesthetic argument

```{r}
tm_shape(mpsz_pop2020) +
  tm_fill(c("YOUNG", "AGED"),
          style = "equal",
          palette = "Blues") +
  tm_layout(legend.position = c("right", "bottom")) +
  tm_borders(alpha = 0.5) +
  tm_style("white")
```

#### 21.4.9.2 Multiple small maps using `tm_polygons()`

```{r}
tm_shape(mpsz_pop2020) +
  tm_polygons(
    c("DEPENDENCY", "AGED"),
    style = c("equal", "quantile"),
    palette = list("Blues", "Greens")
  ) +
  tm_layout(legend.position = c("right", "bottom"))
```

#### 21.4.9.3 Group-by variable using `tm_facets()`

```{r}
tm_shape(mpsz_pop2020) +
  tm_fill(
    "DEPENDENCY",
    style = "quantile",
    palette = "Blues",
    midpoint = NA
  ) +
  tm_facets(by = "REGION_N", free.coords = TRUE) +
  tm_layout(
    legend.show = FALSE,
    title.position = c("center", "center"),
    title.size = 1.0
  ) +
  tm_borders(alpha = 0.5)
```

**Final reflection:** Across all versions, the same data can give different impressions depending on classification and layout. This exercise reminded me that choropleth mapping is not just about plotting values. It is also about choosing a design that supports a fair visual comparison.
